definition: A named block of code that takes input and hands back a result. Every kata asks you to finish one.
The keyword that hands a value back out of a function. Without it, the function gives back nothing.
A named input a function receives, like `name` in greet(name). You use it inside the function like a variable.
The actual value you pass into a function when calling it: in greet('Tyler'), the argument is 'Tyler'.
Text — a word, a sentence, anything in quotes: "hello". The letter s in prompts usually means a string.
A whole number, like 5 or -3. The letter n in prompts usually means a number.
A True/False value. Python capitalizes them: True, False.
A name that holds a value, like x = 5. The value can change; the name stays.
An ordered collection in square brackets: [1, 2, 3]. Items keep their order and can repeat.
A collection of key-value pairs in braces: {"a": 1}. You look values up by key, like a real dictionary.
A position in a list or string. Python counts from 0, so s[0] is the first character; s[-1] is the last.
A function that lives on a value, called with a dot: s.upper() is the string s's upper method.
The remainder after division: 7 % 2 is 1. n % 2 == 0 is the classic even-number test.
A decision in code: run one branch when a condition is true, another when it isn't.
Code that repeats: for item in items runs once per item; while runs until a condition stops being true.
A variable that builds up an answer as a loop runs — like total += n each time around.
A function that calls itself on a smaller piece of the problem, until it hits a case simple enough to answer directly.
The stopping condition of a recursive function — the input so simple it's answered without recursing again.
To run a function: add(2, 3) calls add with 2 and 3.
The grammar rules of the language. A SyntaxError means Python couldn't read your code — often a missing : or quote.
Python's raw error report. Scary-looking but just a trail of where the error happened; the last line is the actual error.
In terminal challenges: a secret string like SQ{...} that proves you completed the task. Find it, paste it, verify.
The text window where you type commands to the computer directly. Terminal challenges are done there, not in this app.
A folder. Directories contain files and other directories.
A small, focused coding exercise — one idea, a few tests, quick to repeat until it's muscle memory.
A previously-passed exercise resurfacing so the skill doesn't decay. Passing it pushes it further into the future.
Consecutive days you've cleared the daily queue. The commit history is the proof.
